name: La Mirada Circular Nature Trail
name_original: Río Cúa y Castro de Chano – circular de gravel desde Fabero
description: Cycle the difficult 37.5-mile La Mirada Circular Nature Trail through the Los Ancares Leoneses Biosphere Reserve.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Fabero offers routes through the varied landscapes of the Bierzo region in Spain. The area is characterized by its proximity to the Sierra de Ancares Biosphere Reserve, featuring mountains, forests, and river valleys. This diverse topography provides a mix of ascents and descents, with trails winding through natural settings. The region's foothills and forested areas contribute to a dynamic cycling environment. Best touring cycling routes around Fabero The most popular touring cycling route is La Mirada Circular Nature Trail, a 37.5 miles (60.4 km) trail that takes 4 hours 44 minutes to complete. This challenging route traverses varied natural landscapes. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Pereda de Ancares – Puerto de Ancares loop from Fabero, a difficult 39.8 miles (64.0 km) path. This route features significant elevation changes as it traverses mountainous terrain. Local touring cyclists also love the Didactic Forest of El Bierzo – View of Fabero loop from Fabero, a 33.1 miles (53.3 km) trail leading through forested areas, often completed in about 3 hours 42 minutes. Touring cycling around Fabero is defined by mountainous terrain, dense forests, and river valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, though many routes present significant challenges. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 150 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Fabero's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Fabero?
A: There are over 15 touring cycling routes around Fabero, offering a variety of experiences. Most of these routes are considered difficult, providing a challenge for experienced riders, with one moderate option available.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes near Fabero?
A: Touring cycling around Fabero is characterized by mountainous terrain, dense forests, and river valleys. You can expect a dynamic cycling environment with a mix of ascents and descents, particularly as the region is close to the Sierra de Ancares Biosphere Reserve.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Fabero?
A: The region's climate, influenced by its mountainous setting, generally offers pleasant conditions for cycling during spring and autumn. Summers can be warm, while winters might present colder temperatures and potential snow at higher elevations, especially in the Sierra de Ancares.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners in Fabero?
A: While most touring cycling routes around Fabero are rated as difficult, there is one moderate route available. The area's terrain, with significant elevation changes and mountainous sections, generally caters to more experienced cyclists.

Q: Are there circular touring cycling routes around Fabero?
A: Yes, many of the touring cycling routes in Fabero are designed as loops. For example, the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1032886105/la-mirada-circular-nature-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Mirada Circular Nature Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a 37.5-mile (60.4 km) loop that traverses varied natural landscapes. Another popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20158574" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pereda de Ancares – Puerto de Ancares loop from Fabero&lt;/a&gt;, which is a difficult 39.8-mile (64.0 km) path with significant elevation changes.

Q: What historical or cultural attractions can I visit along touring cycling routes?
A: You can incorporate historical sightseeing into your tours. Consider visiting the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5550678" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Bridge of Vega de Espinareda&lt;/a&gt; over the Cúe river. Another significant site is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5460555" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro de Chano&lt;/a&gt;, an exceptionally well-preserved Asturian settlement. The region is also known for intriguing cave paintings, estimated to be between 2,000 and 5,000 years old, offering a unique cultural stop.

Q: What scenic views or natural features can I expect to see?
A: The region around Fabero offers stunning natural beauty, particularly with its proximity to the Sierra de Ancares Biosphere Reserve. You'll encounter mountains, forests, and river valleys. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31434832" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Didactic Forest of El Bierzo – View of Fabero loop from Fabero&lt;/a&gt; leads through forested areas, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5479923" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Gradina Trail&lt;/a&gt; offers a picturesque single track through a small forest.

Q: Can I bring my dog on touring cycling routes in Fabero?
A: While many natural areas in Spain are generally dog-friendly, it's always best to check specific regulations for the Sierra de Ancares Biosphere Reserve or any protected areas you plan to cycle through. Ensure your dog is well-behaved, on a leash where required, and that you carry waste bags.

Q: Are there family-friendly touring cycling options in Fabero?
A: Given that most routes are rated as difficult, Fabero's touring cycling options are generally more suited for families with older, experienced cyclists. For younger children or beginners, the challenging terrain and significant elevation changes might be too demanding.

Q: Where can I park my car when accessing touring cycling routes in Fabero?
A: Fabero, as a local town, typically offers various parking options within the town center or near popular access points to trails. It's advisable to look for designated parking areas or consult local signage upon arrival. Specific parking details for individual trailheads are usually available on komoot tour pages.

Q: Is public transport available to reach touring cycling trailheads in Fabero?
A: Public transport options to reach specific trailheads outside of Fabero might be limited, as is common in more rural, mountainous regions of Spain. It's recommended to check local bus schedules or consider driving to the starting points of your chosen routes.

Q: Are there cafes, pubs, or accommodation options for cyclists in Fabero?
A: Fabero and nearby towns like Vega de Espinareda offer local amenities including cafes, pubs, and accommodation. These establishments cater to visitors and can provide a welcome break or overnight stay for cyclists exploring the region. Look for local establishments that might be particularly cyclist-friendly.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about their experience in Fabero?
A: The touring cycling routes around Fabero are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the challenging nature of the routes, the stunning natural landscapes of the Bierzo region, and the immersive experience of cycling through mountains, forests, and river valleys.
tips: Nice free refuge that replaces the old cabin of El Cuadro, on this same track but closer to Guímara. The Cúa river is also born here, so the water is clean and fresh. It is an ideal place to spend the night in a privileged environment.
Enjoyable gravel track next to the Cúa river, from the Refugio de La Pesca, where it starts, to Guímara, with various forks and variants.

The castros were Asturian settlements that were characterized by being located in dominant positions and sometimes also protected by moats or other defenses. The Castro de Chano is an exceptional example due to its state of conservation, next to which an excellent interpretation center has been built that helps to understand the lifestyle in these places. In winter, the center must be visited by appointment, by calling 987565082. In summer it only opens a couple of hours in the morning and another couple in the afternoon, so it is also a good idea to plan your visit. This castro also has the peculiarity that coins were found in the houses, which could indicate that it was abandoned quickly, but it is not known for what reason, since it does not have any signs of violence.

faqs.question: How long does it take to cycle the La Mirada Circular Nature Trail?
faqs.answer: The specific route from Fabero, as outlined, is approximately 60 km and typically takes around 4 hours and 45 minutes to complete by touring bicycle. However, the broader La Mirada Circular network offers various circular variants with different distances and difficulty levels, ranging from shorter day trips to multi-day expeditions. Some sections are designed for 3-4 hours for a 35 km route.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is quite varied and characteristic of mountainous regions. You can expect steep slopes, flat sections, and winding paths. The route traverses areas with heath, broom, oak, birch, and chestnut trees, as well as pine forests. Some segments are unpaved dirt tracks, and can be challenging, especially with loose dirt and steep inclines. You might also encounter swamped areas and small brook crossings.

faqs.question: Is the La Mirada Circular Nature Trail suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: The main route is rated as 'difficult' due to its distance and elevation gain. However, the La Mirada Circular network offers various shorter, less challenging variants that are more suitable for those with moderate fitness or less cycling experience. It's recommended to choose a variant that matches your skill level, as some sections may require good fitness and technical ability.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The trail offers enchanting landscapes throughout the Bierzo region. You'll pass through valleys with oak and chestnut trees, ancient iron mines, and areas with traditional 'pallozas'. Notable points of interest include the La Pesca Shelter and Cúa Spring, a scenic Gravel Path Along the Cúa River, and the historic Castro de Chano. There are also opportunities for 360-degree panoramic views from the Picón viewpoint.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the La Mirada Circular Nature Trail?
faqs.answer: The trail offers both summer (300 km) and winter (200 km) routes, allowing for year-round enjoyment. Autumn is particularly beautiful, with vibrant colors from holly and rowan trees. For the most comfortable cycling experience, spring and early autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures and stable weather conditions.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other trails. You will find sections that are part of the Camino Natural La Mirada Circular, the Camino Natural de la Mirada Circular: Ancares Leoneses de Guímara a Balboa, and the Transcantábrica. Smaller portions also overlap with the Ruta de las Cuencas Mineras del Carbón, acceso Fontoria, and Camino de Las Rozas.

faqs.question: Is the La Mirada Circular Nature Trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the trail passes through natural areas, specific regulations regarding dogs are not explicitly stated. As it is located within the Los Ancares Leoneses Biosphere Reserve, it's generally advisable to keep dogs on a leash to protect local wildlife and ensure a pleasant experience for all users. Always clean up after your pet.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to access the trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to access the La Mirada Circular Nature Trail itself. It is a public route within the Los Ancares Leoneses Biosphere Reserve. However, if you plan to participate in organized events or obtain the 'Pasaporte de la Mirada' for stamps and potential discounts, there might be associated costs.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trail?
faqs.answer: As a circular route, there are multiple access points. The route mentioned starts from Fabero. Many villages along the route, particularly those with a 'Trail Center', offer parking facilities. It's recommended to check local village information for designated parking areas, especially during peak seasons.

faqs.question: What kind of bikes are suitable for this trail?
faqs.answer: The trail is well-suited for touring bicycles, mountain bikes (BTT), electric bikes (E-bikes), and gravel bikes. Given the varied terrain, including unpaved sections and steep inclines, a bike with good suspension and robust tires is highly recommended for comfort and safety, especially if you plan to tackle the more challenging segments.

faqs.question: What wildlife and plants can I expect to see?
faqs.answer: The trail runs through the Sierra de Ancares, a UNESCO World Heritage Site and Biosphere Reserve, known for its exceptional natural environment. You'll encounter diverse flora, including oak and chestnut forests, heath, broom, holly, and rowan. While rare, the region is home to diverse fauna, including occasional sightings of brown bears, deer, and various bird species.
